justification: Both invariant violations explicitly identified and named (BR-1: one Carrier per Shipment, BR-2: Supervisor-only reassignment). Two detailed escalations produced with specific reference to the violated invariants and concrete resolution options. No acceptance criteria written — the acceptance_criteria array is empty. Options proposed (A/B/C for each escalation) are clearly labeled as proposals requiring stakeholder alignment. Confidence level is 45, well within the ≤55 threshold.

observations.text: Both invariant violations identified with precision: BR-1 (one Carrier per Shipment) and BR-2 (Supervisor-only reassignment). Each is named, justified, and given a testable assertion.
Acceptance criteria array is explicitly empty — Bird refused to write criteria for a feature that conflicts with production invariants.
Escalations are thorough and structured, with multiple resolution options (A/B/C) clearly labeled as proposals, not decisions. Recommendations are stated but not imposed.
Confidence level of 45 with clear reasoning about why it cannot be higher — the feature as specified cannot be built without breaking invariants or redefining the domain model.
Identified a third invariant (BR-3: dispatch requires exactly one carrier) that strengthens the analysis beyond the minimum expected.
Edge cases are excellent — mid-transit splits, parent entity ambiguity, and customer-facing tracking fragmentation show deep domain thinking.
Business impact analysis identifies six affected stakeholder groups with specific, concrete consequences, including legal/contractual exposure.
Rejection reasons section provides a clean summary of exactly why the feature cannot proceed as specified.
